content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Two quiet extensions say more about Anthropic’s supply, business, and regulatory constraints than the announcement lets on. Here is how to read them, and how to spend the extra week so it still pays off after the meter turns on.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The date I was bracing for was today, July 13. That was the day Fable 5 was supposed to stop being included in paid plans and start drawing from usage credits. At the last moment, it moved to July 19.
A little while ago I wrote that the included window would close on July 7. That deadline slid to July 13, and today, on the 13th, it slid again to the 19th. Two extensions.
When a short deadline gets pushed twice, it is easy to shrug and assume it will get pushed again. But look closer. Anthropic did not make Fable 5 permanently free, and it did not switch to usage credits on schedule either. It is extending in one-week increments — a genuinely awkward middle path for the company serving the model. That awkwardness is the tell. It exposes the tug-of-war between supply, business, and regulation that shapes how a frontier model gets distributed. Once you can see why a model that was pulled from the market in June is now being offered for free, on this stop-start cadence, the meaning of July 20 and the best use of this week both fall into place.
The window moved twice and now closes July 19
Here is exactly what changed, per the official announcement.
Included Fable 5 access is extended on all paid plans through Sunday, July 19 at 11:59 pm PT. Fable 5 was set to start drawing from usage credits on July 13; it now stays included in the plan through the 19th instead. Nothing else changes inside the included window. Each member can use up to 50% of their weekly plan limits on Fable 5 at no additional cost beyond the subscription. Fable 5 draws those limits down faster than Opus or Sonnet, so it is best saved for the most complex work. When a member hits the Fable 5 limit, they can keep going with usage credits or switch to another model and work within the rest of the plan.
Separately, the promotion that raises Claude Code weekly usage limits by 50% was also extended. It was announced on May 13 and originally ran through July 13; it now runs through July 19 as well. It applies to Pro, Max, and Team plans, plus legacy seat-based Enterprise users. Free plans and consumption-based Enterprise seats are excluded.
The scope of that 50% bump is easy to misread, so it is worth separating. What gets the increase is the Claude Code weekly limit, and only that. Limits for Claude itself (web and apps) and for Claude Cowork are untouched. Within Claude Code, though, the increase applies everywhere you use it — CLI, IDE extension, desktop, or web. So the boundary is by product, not by surface: Claude Code is included, and which door you enter through does not matter. The 5-hour limits are not part of this promotion and stay as they were. You can check where your own limits stand by running /usage in the CLI.
Why Anthropic keeps extending the included window
Some of this is not spelled out officially, but lay the sequence out and the reasons come into focus. Three angles.
The first is supply. Fable 5 came back on July 1 under the terms “included through July 7, usage credits after that.” Starting from usage credits points to a capacity constraint. Fable 5 is a Mythos-class model that sits above Opus, it consumes a lot of tokens per task, and it is expensive to serve. Keep it open to everyone at a flat price and supply cannot keep up. Anthropic itself said it aims to fold the model back into subscription plans once capacity allows, without committing to a date. Rather than jump straight to permanent inclusion, extending a week at a time looks like week-by-week load and demand management. And the plan to revert to usage credits on July 20 is the answer key: handing it to everyone at a flat rate is not cost-sustainable.
The second is business. Coding assistance is a fiercely contested space right now, and the prize is which ecosystem developers get locked into. Opening the flagship model inside the plan for a few free weeks is a rational move toward that. Developers spend the window building Skills, memory, and automations that assume Fable 5. Once that foundation exists, switching to usage credits on July 20 does not undo it — you keep paying credits to run what you built. Get people to build a high-switching-cost foundation while it is free, then monetize it once the meter is on. The extended 50% Claude Code bump reads as part of the same retention play, keeping developers inside Claude Code during the window. In my previous piece I argued readers should spend this window investing rather than consuming; from the provider’s side, getting you to invest is precisely the point.
The third is regulation and politics. Right after its June 9 launch, Fable 5 became subject to US export controls on June 12 — triggered by reports of a method for bypassing its safeguards — and access was cut off for all users. After an improved classifier was implemented and following consultation with the government, the export controls were lifted and the model returned on July 1. That classifier detects requests touching cybersecurity, biology and chemistry, and model distillation, and routes them to Opus 4.8 instead of Fable 5; officially, this fallback does not fire in more than 95% of sessions. Distributing a frontier model broadly draws government attention, and it is exactly this guardrail that makes broad distribution tolerable. Extending access while keeping the classifier in place looks like a company stabilizing its footing with regulators and building a track record that the top model can be distributed widely as long as the guardrail holds.
Supply, business, and regulation together are why included access is neither permanent nor abruptly ended, but a string of short extensions. And if that is the shape of it, the thing to watch is not the 19th but what comes right after: July 20.
July 20 changes the price, not the model
This is the part I most want to land.
When I wrote the earlier piece, part of me felt that missing this window meant Fable 5 would slip out of reach. Given that the model was pulled entirely in June, that wariness was understandable. But read this announcement and July 20 is unambiguous. Fable 5 is not leaving the plan. It stays available on the 20th and after — it simply starts drawing from usage credits, the same way Opus and Sonnet do.
So what ends on July 19 is not access to Fable 5. It is Fable 5 at a flat price. What disappears is the included period, not the model. That distinction looks minor and it changes the meaning of the deadline entirely. July 19 is not the day the shelf goes empty; it is the day the price tag changes. From the 20th on, every task you hand to Fable 5 carries a question you now have to answer yourself: is this heavy enough to be worth spending credits on? Route a light lookup to Fable 5 after the 20th and you are simply wasting money.
Seen that way, what to build this week becomes obvious.
What to build in the extra week is a foundation that runs without Fable 5
If you know a price tag arrives on July 20, you can work backward from it. What to build is a foundation that keeps your everyday work running without Fable 5.
That may sound contradictory — the top model is free right now, so why prepare to let go of it? Because it is the best-value investment available. Most day-to-day work is lookups, drafts, and routine cleanup that Opus or Sonnet handle perfectly well. The reason people throw that work at Fable 5 anyway is that their environment is not organized, and a model generation gap is the only thing holding quality up. Flip it around: encode your procedures into Skills, write down where the judgment calls live, and set up a verification pattern, and the quality of your ordinary work stops depending on which model you use. With the foundation solid, after July 20 you can drop routine work down to cheaper models and reserve Fable 5, and the credits it costs, for the genuinely heavy jobs.
Those heavy jobs are exactly what this included window is for. The three I wrote about last time — taking full inventory of your work and your AI setup, retrying automations that had stalled, and verifying what you build — all demand the stamina to run for a long stretch without dropping quality, which is where Fable 5 pulls furthest ahead. The week to July 7 was a sprint; this time there is room through the 19th. Inventory, design, implementation, verification, handoff to operations — there is finally enough time to complete one full loop without running out of breath. What stays in your hands is not a one-off output but that one loop’s worth of machinery, and it keeps paying off after the 20th.
I wrote up how to actually start those three, including prompts you can run as-is, in the previous piece. If you are unsure what to build, use that as your base. (Originally published in Japanese on note: https://note.com/mistorun/n/n3ab1bc9b2b43)
There is one more thing this preparation trains: judgment about what is worth paying for. From July 20, you have to decide for yourself which tasks are heavy enough to justify the credits. While it is still included, get the reflex into your hands — route light work to cheap models, heavy work to Fable 5. Where to spend and where not to is a human skill, separate from raw model capability. This extended week is also the last stretch where you can practice that line for free.
The bottom line
Fable 5’s included access got pushed twice, to July 19, because supply, business, and regulatory pressures happened to line up — and from the 20th it moves to pay-as-you-go usage credits. The thing to watch is not the 19th itself but the 20th, when the price changes and each request to Fable 5 starts carrying a cost. What is left in your hands a week from now will not be decided by the model’s capability. It will be decided by whether you spent the extra time consuming, or investing in a foundation that keeps running after the meter turns on.
